chain_of_thought: Stack shows scudo allocator abort on deallocate, with first app frame sub70 in libaoecore.so, called from fuzz_one_input. No direct frame names for memcpy, etc. Ghidra tools for this binary are currently erroring (404) when listing/searching functions, so I cannot decompile sub70 or walk dataflow; I only know its symbol name from lib map. JNI bridge Java_com_google_android_decode_AoeUtils_ae corresponds to AoeUtils.ae(byte[]):byte[]; fuzz harness reaches sub70 directly (not through JNI in this trace), so this particular crash is from native fuzzer, not the Java entry. Scudo header corruption at free usually comes from heap metadata overwrite (OOB write, UAF, double free) in app code, but could also be due to harness feeding invalid pointers or violating allocator contract. Because I cannot inspect implementation of sub70 or its callers, I cannot confirm whether it misuses heap with attacker-controlled sizes/indices vs. the fuzzer freeing invalid memory. However, the presence of a dedicated fuzz_one_input wrapper suggests a libFuzzer-style harness that passes a data blob into libaoecore, making heap corruption plausibly driven by input. No evidence that this is an intentional abort or safe guardrail. Overall I lean toward real memory corruption but with moderate confidence due to missing code view and absence of exact operation (no memcpy frame, etc.). Severity is high if real (heap metadata corruption implies potential exploitation).
reasons: Crash is scudo allocator header-corruption abort, indicating heap metadata corruption (likely OOB write, UAF, or double free) in libaoecore.so
Crashing frame is sub70 in libaoecore.so, not a sanitizer itself, implying bug in application/library code rather than allocator
Native library is exercised via a fuzz harness (fuzz_one_input), strongly suggesting operations depend on attacker-controlled input, making heap misuse realistically triggerable
No evidence that the abort is a deliberate guard; instead it is a generic allocator consistency failure
recommendations: Re-analyze libaoecore.so with working Ghidra session, decompile sub70 and its callers, and identify heap operations (alloc/free/memcpy) that can be influenced by fuzz input
Run the existing fuzz harness under ASan/UBSan with symbols to collect detailed stack traces and reproduce the header corruption, then pinpoint the exact misuse (double free, UAF, or OOB)
Add strict bounds checks and lifetime/ownership tracking around all dynamic allocations in the affected code paths, and add regression tests for the reproducer input
assumptions: fuzz_one_input passes attacker-controlled data into sub70 or deeper library routines
The scudo header corruption is not due to an artificial fault injected by the harness itself
JNI usage of the same native routines would expose similar behaviour under crafted app inputs
limitations: Ghidra MCP for libaoecore.so is currently returning 404 for function listing/search, preventing direct decompilation of sub70
Exact heap misuse pattern (OOB vs UAF vs double free) is unknown from the provided trace
I cannot see how Java_com_google_android_decode_AoeUtils_ae maps to native routines inside libaoecore.so or whether additional validation exists there
